content.text: When an AI agent hallucinates during an on-call incident, misroutes a critical escalation, or floods PagerDuty with false-positive alerts, the people who feel this most directly are site reliability engineers (SREs) and support operators. Their experiences — the runbooks that don't cover AI-specific failure modes, the alert thresholds calibrated for stateless services that don't account for non-deterministic model outputs, the on-call rotations carrying the cognitive load of novel failure types — are a rich, underutilized source of improvement signal.
Operator feedback from SRE and support teams
is the discipline of systematically capturing, structuring, and routing that signal into engineering backlogs and process improvements.

attrs.alt: SRE and support operator feedback channels: SRE Feedback Types → Support Escalation → AI Post-Mortem Template → Engineering Handoff, with feedback loop cadence at bottom

content.text: What It Is

Operator feedback collection for AI systems is the set of channels, templates, and processes that turn SRE and support operator experiences into structured improvement tasks. It covers three feedback streams: (1)
SRE operational feedback
— alert noise, runbook gaps, on-call pain points; (2)
support escalation taxonomy
— a severity classification scheme designed for AI-specific failure modes; and (3)
structured post-mortems
— incident retrospectives extended with AI-specific sections covering root cause in the model/data/infra stack.
Why It Matters

SREs are the first to detect systemic AI failures that don't manifest as classic errors: elevated latency from token budget exhaustion, subtle cost spikes from runaway tool-call loops, or a model that starts refusing valid requests after a policy update.

Support operators handle escalations that reveal AI behavior patterns invisible in automated metrics. An operator who handles ten escalations about the AI misinterpreting a specific document type holds critical pattern knowledge.
Without structured collection, operator feedback is informal — shared in Slack, forgotten in meeting notes, never actioned. Structured collection converts it into tickets with owners.

Post-mortems that don't have AI-specific sections consistently misattribute root cause: an LLM hallucination gets labeled as a "data issue" and the eval suite is never updated to catch the same failure again.
SRE Feedback Types
Operational Pain Points
Alert fatigue reports
: SREs logging which alerts fired but were not actionable. AI systems tend to produce spiky, non-stationary metrics that trigger static threshold alerts incorrectly.
Runbook gap tickets

: incidents where the on-call SRE had to improvise because no runbook covered the failure type. AI-specific failures (e.g., "model started refusing all requests in a category after a system prompt update") are commonly undocumented.
On-call pain point log

: a weekly structured write-up from the outgoing on-call engineer covering the most painful aspects of the rotation. For AI systems, this often surfaces: missing observability ("I couldn't tell if the model was actually responding or just timing out"), noisy alerts, and unclear escalation paths for novel failure modes.
Performance Signals

Latency spike patterns: SREs document when latency spikes occur correlated with specific request patterns (long context windows, high tool-call counts, time-of-day). This is structural knowledge that doesn't appear in dashboards.
Tool call failure rates: when an AI agent's tool calls fail at elevated rates (rate limit errors, timeouts, malformed parameters), SREs are often first to notice via alert or user escalation.
Cost overrun signals: unexpected token usage spikes — often caused by prompt injection, runaway loops, or context window bloat — detected first by cost anomaly alerts.
Support Escalation Taxonomy for AI Incidents

Standard severity levels (SEV-1 through SEV-5) need AI-specific sub-categories to route escalations correctly. A generic "model returning errors" ticket looks like a SEV-4 application error; it might actually be a SEV-2 systematic hallucination affecting critical workflows.
Severity
Description
AI-Specific Examples
Response SLA
SEV-1
Data loss or security breach
PII leakage from context window; jailbreak producing harmful content
15 min
SEV-2
Full AI system unavailable or systematically incorrect
All responses refused; systematic hallucination in critical category
30 min
SEV-3
Elevated error rate >5% or significant quality degradation
Tool call failure rate >10%; latency p99 >10s; cost 3× projected
2 hours
SEV-4
Elevated error rate or quality issue without broad impact
Hallucination in one query category; alert noise; runbook gap
Next business day
SEV-5
Cosmetic or performance issue with no user impact
Response formatting inconsistency; slightly elevated latency
Sprint backlog
Structured AI Post-Mortem Template

Post-mortems for AI systems need sections beyond standard infrastructure post-mortems. The root cause may lie in the model itself (a capability regression after a provider model update), in the prompting layer (a system prompt change that caused unexpected refusals), or in the retrieval layer (a stale vector index returning wrong context). Without explicit sections for each, the analysis defaults to infrastructure and misses the actual cause.

# AI Incident Post-Mortem

## Incident Summary
- **Severity**: SEV-[1-5]
- **Start time**: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM UTC
- **End time**: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM UTC
- **MTTD** (Mean Time to Detect): X minutes
- **MTTR** (Mean Time to Resolve): X minutes
- **Affected query count**: N
- **AI incident category**: [hallucination | refusal | tool-call-fail | context-lost | latency-spike | cost-spike | security]

## Timeline
| Time (UTC) | Event |
|-----------|-------|
| HH:MM | First alert fired |
| HH:MM | On-call acknowledged |
| HH:MM | Root cause identified |
| HH:MM | Mitigation applied |
| HH:MM | Incident resolved |

## Root Cause Analysis
- **Layer**: [model | prompting | retrieval | tool | infrastructure]
- **Root cause description**: ...
- **Contributing factors**: ...
- **Was a model/provider update involved?**: Y/N + details
- **Was a system prompt change involved?**: Y/N + details

## Detection
- How was this detected? [alert | user report | SRE observation]
- Were existing alerts sufficient? If not, what was missing?

## Remediation
- Immediate mitigation taken:
- Permanent fix applied (or planned):

## Action Items
| Item | Owner | Due Date | Priority |
|------|-------|----------|----------|
| Add eval test for this failure mode | [name] | YYYY-MM-DD | P1 |
| Update runbook for [category] failures | [name] | YYYY-MM-DD | P2 |
| Tune alert threshold for [metric] | [name] | YYYY-MM-DD | P2 |

## Lessons Learned
- What went well:
- What could be improved:
Feedback-to-Engineering Handoff Process
SRE or support operator files a structured ticket (Jira, Linear) using the AI incident template — not a freeform note.
The on-call engineering rotation reviews new SEV-3+ tickets daily; SEV-4/5 tickets go into the weekly triage meeting.
Each ticket is assigned an owner and a category tag from the AI incident taxonomy.
For hallucination and quality failures, the assigned engineer adds an eval test that would have caught the failure before closing the ticket.
For runbook gaps, the engineer updates the runbook and links the ticket to the updated doc.
The SRE who filed the ticket is notified when the action items close — same "close the loop" principle as the KB feedback loop.
Tooling Integration

# Example: PagerDuty → Jira AI incident ticket automation
import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request

app = FastAPI()

AI_INCIDENT_TEMPLATE_ID = "jira-template-id-123"

@app.post("/webhooks/pagerduty")
async def on_pagerduty_alert(request: Request):
    payload = await request.json()
    incident = payload.get("incident", {})
    severity = incident.get("severity", "sev4")  # p1/p2/p3/p4 in PagerDuty terms

    # Only auto-create Jira tickets for SEV-3 and above
    pd_to_sev = {"p1": "SEV-1", "p2": "SEV-2", "p3": "SEV-3"}
    if severity not in pd_to_sev:
        return {"status": "skipped", "reason": "below threshold"}

    jira_severity = pd_to_sev[severity]
    await create_jira_ai_incident_ticket(
        summary=f"[{jira_severity}] AI Incident: {incident.get('title', 'Unknown')}",
        description=build_postmortem_template(incident, jira_severity),
        priority=jira_severity,
        pd_incident_id=incident.get("id"),
    )
    return {"status": "ticket_created"}

async def create_jira_ai_incident_ticket(summary, description, priority, pd_incident_id):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        await client.post(
            "https://your-org.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/issue",
            json={
                "fields": {
                    "summary": summary,
                    "description": {"type": "doc", "version": 1, "content": [{"type": "paragraph", "content": [{"type": "text", "text": description}]}]},
                    "issuetype": {"name": "AI Incident"},
                    "priority": {"name": priority},
                    "labels": ["ai-incident", f"pagerduty-{pd_incident_id}"],
                }
            },
            headers={"Authorization": "Bearer YOUR_JIRA_TOKEN"},
        )
Benefits
Early detection of AI-specific failures
: SREs notice latency spikes and quality degradation in production before they appear in weekly metrics reviews or customer escalations.
Root cause accuracy
: structured AI-specific post-mortems correctly attribute root cause to the model, prompt, retrieval, or tool layer — not generically to "infrastructure."
Accumulated institutional memory
: each post-mortem builds a record of how AI failures present and how they are resolved, reducing diagnosis time for future incidents.
Eval suite growth
: requiring an eval test addition for every SEV-2 and SEV-3 incident means the test suite grows with each incident, providing increasing regression protection over time.
On-call experience improvement
: pain point logs and runbook updates from operator feedback reduce on-call cognitive load with each iteration.
Trade-offs
Meeting overhead
: structured post-mortems and triage cadences add recurring meetings. For small teams running a single AI feature, the overhead may exceed the value — use lighter-weight async write-ups instead.
Template fatigue
: a complex post-mortem template that must be filled out for every SEV-4 incident creates resistance. Apply full templates to SEV-2 and SEV-3; use abbreviated templates for SEV-4 and SEV-5.
Blameless culture dependency
: post-mortems only work in a blameless culture. If the process is used to assign blame rather than find systemic causes, operators will stop contributing honest feedback.
Performance and Scalability

The feedback collection infrastructure (PagerDuty webhooks, Jira ticket creation, Slack channel posting) must be reliable even during incidents. Use dedicated service accounts for automation with rate-limit handling — during a major incident, PagerDuty may fire tens of alerts per minute, and Jira ticket creation must not block the alert routing pipeline.

For post-mortem document storage, use a version-controlled wiki (Confluence, Notion, or GitHub markdown) rather than shared drives. Version control gives an audit trail of edits and allows rollback if inaccurate information is added post-incident.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Structured AI post-mortem + escalation taxonomy (this pattern)
Correct root cause attribution; drives eval suite growth; builds runbooks
Overhead; requires blameless culture; template maintenance
Generic infrastructure post-mortem only
Familiar; low overhead
Misattributes AI failures; never updates eval suite
Informal Slack discussion only
Zero process overhead; immediate
No action items; knowledge lost; patterns invisible across incidents
Automated anomaly detection only
Catches metric deviations 24/7
Misses operational pain (runbook gaps, on-call experience); no root cause
Common Mistakes
Generic post-mortem templates
: using an infrastructure post-mortem template for AI incidents consistently produces "root cause: unknown" or "root cause: data issue" when the actual root cause was a model capability regression.
No eval test requirement
: closing AI incident tickets without adding an eval test means the same failure will recur undetected after a future model update.
Alert threshold copy-pasted from non-AI services
: AI inference latency is non-stationary (context length, tool call count affect latency strongly). Static thresholds calibrated for stateless services fire constantly.
No AI-specific severity category
: without
hallucination
and
refusal
as named categories, these failures get miscategorized as generic bugs and routed to the wrong team.
Best Practices

Require an eval test addition for every SEV-2 and SEV-3 AI incident. This is the highest-leverage action that comes from a post-mortem — it encodes the failure into the test suite so it never recurs silently.
Rotate SREs through the on-call AI rotation with AI-specific onboarding: what hallucination looks like in metrics, how to use model provider status pages, how to roll back a system prompt.
Run a monthly "alert quality" review: for each alert that fired in the last 30 days, classify it as actionable, noisy, or missing. Tune thresholds based on the outcome.
Establish a
#ai-incidents
Slack channel with PagerDuty and Jira integrations — incidents are automatically posted so the entire engineering team can observe patterns over time.
Security and Reliability
Post-mortem access control
: SEV-1 post-mortems may contain details about security vulnerabilities. Restrict access to the security team and relevant engineers until CVEs are patched.
Incident ticket PII
: support escalation tickets often contain customer data. Strip PII before linking tickets to engineering backlogs or sharing in public Slack channels.
Runbook versioning
: store runbooks in version control so that changes are auditable and can be rolled back if an update introduces an error in the remediation procedure.
Mental Model
SREs and support operators are
the immune system of the production AI system

. They detect pathogens (failures) that automated metrics miss, carry memory of past incidents, and know which parts of the system are most fragile. The post-mortem template and escalation taxonomy are the
antibody catalog
— a structured record of what the immune system has learned. Without this catalog, the system re-learns the same lesson after every reinfection.
Key Takeaways
SRE and support operator feedback is a high-value, underutilized signal that surfaces AI-specific failures invisible to automated metrics.
Use an AI-specific severity taxonomy that includes
hallucination
refusal
tool-call-fail
context-lost
, and
cost-spike
— generic categories consistently misroute AI incidents.
Post-mortem templates for AI systems must include the model/prompting/retrieval/tool/infrastructure layer axis to correctly attribute root cause.
Require an eval test addition for every SEV-2 and SEV-3 incident before the ticket is closed.
Automate PagerDuty → Jira ticket creation for SEV-3+ so operator feedback always results in a tracked engineering item.
Run a monthly alert quality review: AI metrics are non-stationary, and alert thresholds calibrated last quarter may be badly tuned today.
Close the loop with operators — notify the person who filed the incident ticket when action items are complete.
